title: Your Roaster Relationship: From Transaction to Partnership
subtitle: Your coffee supplier should be your closest partner, not just another invoice.

toc.label: Why Your Roaster Is Your Most Important Supplier
What to Expect From a Good Roaster

sections.heading: Why Your Roaster Is Your Most Important Supplier
sections.body:
Coffee is your core product. It defines your quality, your identity, and your customer promise. The relationship with the roaster who supplies it is the most consequential supplier relationship in your business.

Yet most cafe owners treat it transactionally. Order beans, receive beans, pay invoice. No feedback loop, no quality discussion, no shared development.

A good roaster-cafe partnership creates:
- **Consistent quality:** The roaster understands your equipment, your water, and your customer preferences, and adjusts the roast profile accordingly.
- **Training support:** Many roasters offer barista training, cupping sessions, and equipment advice as part of the relationship.
- **Problem-solving:** When shots are tasting off, a good roaster helps diagnose whether it is the bean, the roast, or the cafe's process.
- **Menu development:** Seasonal blends, guest coffees, single origins — a roaster who knows your brand can suggest products that enhance your offering.
- **Emergency cover:** When you need an extra delivery mid-week, a roaster who values your relationship makes it happen.

The risk of a purely transactional relationship: when demand is tight, the roaster prioritises their partners over their customers. When quality dips, you have no mechanism to address it. When prices rise, there is no goodwill to negotiate.

sections.heading: What to Expect From a Good Roaster
sections.body:
Not all roasters are equal. Use these criteria to evaluate your current supplier or choose a new one:

**Quality consistency.** The same blend should taste the same week after week. Seasonal variation is normal with single origins, but house blends should be stable. If your shots taste different every delivery, raise it.

**Freshness.** Beans should arrive within 7–14 days of roast date. Ask for roast dates on every bag. If your roaster cannot provide them, question their freshness standards.

**Communication.** A good roaster tells you when a component bean in the blend changes, when the price is increasing and why, and when they are introducing something new. No surprises.

**Training.** At minimum: an initial setup session when you start using their beans (dialling in, recipe development). Ideally: ongoing training for new team members, cupping sessions, equipment advice.

**Delivery reliability.** Orders arrive when promised, in full, undamaged. This sounds basic — but unreliable delivery causes operational chaos.

**Pricing transparency.** You should understand how the price is structured: green bean cost, roasting cost, margin. This makes price negotiations factual, not adversarial.

**Ethical sourcing.** Can the roaster tell you where the beans come from, how the farmers are paid, and what social or environmental initiatives they support? Increasingly, your customers want to know — and so should you.

**Visit their roastery.** If possible, visit. See the operation. Meet the people. Taste with them. This builds relationship depth that emails cannot.

sections.heading: Building the Partnership
sections.body:
A partnership is reciprocal. Here is what you bring to the table:

**Volume commitment.** Reliable, predictable ordering. Tell the roaster your weekly volume and order consistently. This helps their production planning and earns you priority.

**Feedback.** When something tastes exceptional, say so. When something is off, say so with specifics: "The last delivery of the house blend has been running faster than usual — are there any changes to the bean or roast profile?" Specific feedback is invaluable to a roaster.

**Brand visibility.** Showcase their beans. Name the roaster on your menu, display retail bags, share their story on social media. This is free marketing for them and authenticity for you.

**Prompt payment.** Pay on time, every time. A cafe that pays reliably gets better service than one that is always chasing credit.

**Honest communication.** If you are considering switching roasters, say so and explain why. If volume is dropping, explain. Surprises damage relationships; honesty preserves them.

Structure the partnership:
- **Monthly check-in:** 15-minute call or email. How is quality? Any issues? Any new products?
- **Quarterly cupping:** Taste together. Assess the current offering. Discuss seasonal changes.
- **Annual review:** Discuss pricing, volume, terms, and future plans. This is when renegotiation happens — from a position of mutual understanding, not confrontation.

sections.heading: The Backup Plan
sections.body:
Depending on a single roaster with no alternative is a business risk. Supply chain disruptions happen — roaster equipment failure, logistics problems, business closure.

Use T105-01 (Backup Supplier Quick-Reference) to document:

**Primary roaster:** Name, contact, lead time, pricing, minimum order
**Backup roaster:** Name, contact, lead time, pricing, minimum order
**Emergency option:** A local roaster who can supply next-day in a crisis

**How to establish a backup without damaging your primary relationship:**
- Order a small amount from the backup quarterly — enough to stay on their system and test quality
- Be transparent with your primary: "We believe in having contingency suppliers. It doesn't change our commitment to you."
- Test the backup beans on your equipment so you know the recipe adjustments needed if you switch
- Keep the backup's contact details and ordering process documented somewhere accessible (not just in the owner's phone)

**When to consider switching primary roasters:**
- Quality has declined and feedback has not resolved it
- Pricing has risen above market without justification
- Delivery reliability has become a problem
- The relationship has become unresponsive
- Your cafe has evolved and the roaster's product no longer fits your brand

Switching is significant — your regulars will notice a change in the coffee. Do it deliberately, with a transition plan, and communicate it positively: "We're excited to introduce our new house blend from [roaster]."

sections.heading: Bakery and Food Supplier Assessment
sections.body:
The same partnership principles apply to your food suppliers, especially bakery and pastry.

Use T202-01 (Bakery Partner Evaluation Scorecard):

| Criterion | Score (1–5) | Notes |
|-----------|------------|-------|
| Product quality | | |
| Consistency batch-to-batch | | |
| Delivery reliability | | |
| Range and variety | | |
| Pricing competitiveness | | |
| Minimum order flexibility | | |
| Willingness to customise | | |
| Shelf life of products | | |
| Allergen information provided | | |
| Communication and responsiveness | | |

**Bakery-specific considerations:**

- **Delivery timing.** Pastries need to arrive before opening. A supplier who delivers at 10am when your rush is at 8am is not fit for purpose.
- **Product consistency.** The croissant should be the same size, colour, and quality every day. If Tuesday's delivery looks different from Thursday's, there is a production quality issue.
- **Allergen transparency.** Your supplier must provide full allergen information for every product. This is your legal obligation under EU allergen labelling requirements — and it depends on your supplier's honesty.
- **Waste impact.** If your supplier has high minimum orders and you are wasting 30% of each delivery, the pricing is not as competitive as it looks.
- **Local options.** A local bakery may cost slightly more per unit but delivers fresher product, supports the community story, and often offers more flexibility on orders and delivery.

Review food suppliers quarterly using the same scorecard. Share feedback directly — most suppliers want to improve and will respond to specific, constructive criticism.

sections.heading: Supplier Performance Reviews
sections.body:
Use T106-01 (Supplier Scorecard Template) to formalise supplier assessment:

For each key supplier (coffee, milk, bakery, packaging), score quarterly:

**Quality (40% weighting)**
- Product meets agreed specification
- Consistency across deliveries
- Freshness and shelf life adequate

**Reliability (30% weighting)**
- Deliveries on time
- Orders complete (no substitutions or shortages)
- Communication of delays or issues

**Value (20% weighting)**
- Pricing competitive vs. market
- Payment terms reasonable
- Volume discounts available

**Relationship (10% weighting)**
- Responsiveness to queries
- Willingness to problem-solve
- Proactive communication

Weighted score below 3.0 = schedule a review meeting to discuss improvements.
Weighted score below 2.5 = begin evaluating alternatives.
Weighted score below 2.0 = transition to backup supplier.

**The review conversation:**
Share your scorecard with the supplier. "Here's how we've rated you this quarter. Quality is strong at 4.5, but reliability dropped to 2.8 because we had three late deliveries. Can we discuss what happened and how to prevent it?"

This is not adversarial. It is professional. Suppliers respect structured feedback because it gives them clear information to act on. Most will improve. Those that do not are telling you something about their priorities.
